nodes.title: Rigorous comparative research on intervention effectiveness
nodes.text: Systematic evaluation of charitable interventions using RCTs, quasi-experimental methods, and meta-analysis where available

connections.evidence: GiveWell's methodology has been validated by external evaluators and correlates with independent effectiveness assessments. Research quality has improved over 10+ years of iteration.
connections.assumptions: Comparative research can identify effectiveness differences between interventions. External validation provides meaningful quality control. Past performance predicts future research quality.

nodes.title: Evidence-based outreach to high-capacity donors
nodes.text: Focused engagement with individuals who have both capacity and stated interest in maximizing their social impact

connections.evidence: RCT showing 15% increase in effective giving rates sustained at 12 months. Natural experiments show 2-3x higher effective giving rates in areas with EA presence.
connections.assumptions: Randomized trial results generalize to broader population. Effects persist beyond measured timeframes. Natural experiment controls for confounding factors.

nodes.title: Robust monitoring and evaluation infrastructure
nodes.text: Systems for tracking donation flows, career transitions, and intermediate impact indicators with external validation where possible

connections.evidence: Independent evaluation by third parties confirms methodology validity. Public reporting of success rates including failures provides transparency.
connections.assumptions: External evaluators remain independent despite ecosystem connections. Measurement systems capture meaningful indicators of progress. Transparency enables course correction.

nodes.title: Publicly available comparative effectiveness research
nodes.text: Research publications comparing intervention cost-effectiveness with explicit methodology and uncertainty quantification

connections.evidence: Donor surveys indicate 73% credit GiveWell research with donation decisions. However, attribution is self-reported and may reflect post-hoc rationalization.
connections.assumptions: Research quality translates to decision quality. Self-reported attribution reflects genuine causal influence. Information availability improves resource allocation.

nodes.title: Donors with increased effective giving behavior
nodes.text: Individuals who have demonstrably increased their donations to cost-effective interventions following exposure to effectiveness information

connections.evidence: Tracked donation flows show $600M+ directed to GiveWell-recommended organizations. Verification through public records where available.
connections.assumptions: Donation tracking accurately captures influenced giving. Recommended organizations maintain effectiveness at scale. Resources are genuinely additional rather than substituted.

nodes.title: Transparent reporting of outcomes including failures
nodes.text: Public documentation of intervention success rates, cost-effectiveness changes over time, and discontinued programs

connections.evidence: Observable strategy changes in response to evidence: reduced deworming support, increased AI safety investment, added criminal justice reform.
connections.assumptions: Transparency enables effective learning and adaptation. Public reporting creates accountability pressure for improvement. Course corrections improve future performance.

nodes.title: Charitable resource allocation based on comparative effectiveness
nodes.text: Donors making giving decisions with access to rigorous comparative information about intervention effectiveness

connections.evidence: Limited and contested. Some evidence that GiveWell-recommended organizations outperform alternatives, but comparative studies are few and subject to selection bias.
connections.assumptions: Comparative effectiveness information improves allocation decisions. Donors act on available information. Better information leads to better outcomes.

nodes.title: Philanthropic resources flowing to higher-impact interventions
nodes.text: Measurable increase in funding for interventions with stronger evidence bases and cost-effectiveness ratios

connections.evidence: AMF malaria net distribution scaled 500% following GiveWell recommendation. Consistent health outcome improvements in areas with high coverage.
connections.assumptions: Resource redirection is genuinely additional. Scaled interventions maintain effectiveness. Health improvements persist over time.

nodes.title: Adaptive strategy based on performance measurement
nodes.text: Systematic adjustment of approaches based on evidence of success and failure rates

connections.evidence: Observable methodology improvements over time. External validation of evaluation approaches. Documented strategy changes in response to evidence.
connections.assumptions: Learning from failures improves future performance. Measurement systems capture meaningful performance indicators. Adaptation occurs faster than environmental change.

nodes.title: Demonstrably more effective charitable interventions at scale
nodes.text: Interventions that deliver measurably better outcomes per dollar invested, verified through independent evaluation

connections.evidence: Global health interventions show measurable impact: malaria mortality reduction, increased immunization coverage, improved nutritional outcomes in target populations.
connections.assumptions: Measured health improvements translate to broader welfare gains. Intervention effects persist without continued support. Benefits outweigh any negative unintended consequences.

nodes.title: Cost-effective interventions implemented at sufficient scale to address problems
nodes.text: Proven interventions reaching the population scale necessary to make meaningful progress on targeted problems

connections.evidence: 200+ million malaria nets distributed through AMF and similar organizations. Estimated 120,000+ lives saved based on conservative modeling.
connections.assumptions: Scale estimates are accurate. Life-saving estimates account for displacement effects and diminishing returns. Scale achieved is sufficient for meaningful problem reduction.

nodes.title: More rigorous approaches to charitable evaluation and allocation
nodes.text: Systematic methods for comparing interventions that improve over time through learning and external critique

connections.evidence: Mixed and limited evidence. Some improvements in evaluation rigor, but fundamental methodological challenges remain. Unclear whether methodological improvements translate to better outcomes.
connections.assumptions: Better evaluation methods lead to better intervention selection. Methodological improvements compound over time. Evaluation advances transfer to other domains.

nodes.title: Measurable improvements in human welfare and reduced suffering
nodes.text: Demonstrable reductions in preventable deaths, disease burden, and other forms of measurable suffering
